title: The Challenge of Discernment
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Challenge of Discernment - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt
outline: Second lesson in the discernment series
Text: Matthew 10:34-37 — Christ brings a sword, not peace
How does this relate to discernment?
Discerning what matters enough to discuss/defend in family and community
The cost of discernment: family division for the sake of truth
summary: Second lesson in a PM series on discernment. Opening with the challenging words of Matthew 10:34-37 (‘I did not come to bring peace but a sword’), Pastor Lyro explores the relationship between discernment and the willingness to stand for Christ even when it creates tension within families. True discernment requires knowing what truths about God are important enough to distinguish and defend.
transcript:
## Introduction: Discernment and the Sword of the Gospel

Our reading from Matthew 10:34–37 may seem like a strange text for a lesson on discernment:

> *“Do not think that I came to bring peace on earth. I did not come to bring peace but a sword. For I have come to set a man against his father, a daughter against her mother… He who loves father or mother more than Me is not worthy of Me.”* (Matthew 10:34–37)

What does this have to do with discernment? Discernment is a skill that does not tend to make us popular. When we discern biblically — when we look at life from a consistently biblical perspective — we will not be universally liked. This passage reminds us that biblical discernment requires us to make clear and unwavering distinctions between what is good and what is evil, and that will sometimes divide even family.

Last week we saw that lack of discernment points to three unavoidable conclusions: spiritual immaturity, backsliding, and the danger of spiritual death. And on the positive side, discernment is proof of spiritual life, spiritual growth, and spiritual maturity.

Tonight I want to talk about the **challenges of discernment** — the obstacles that keep us from growing in biblical discernment. Discernment is a skill that must be sharpened by practice:

> *“Solid food belongs to those who are of full age, that is, those who by reason of use have their senses exercised to discern both good and evil.”* (Hebrews 5:14)

So what gets in the way?

## Challenge 1: Internal Influences

Our own self gets in the way of growing in discernment. The prophet says:

> *“The heart is deceitful above all things, and desperately wicked; who can know it?”* (Jeremiah 17:9)

We all have a sinful nature from the moment of conception. When we are born again we are given a new heart and a new nature, and yet wickedness continues to dwell within us — that is the whole sanctification process. As Paul says in Romans 7:15–20, the things he knows he should do, he does not, and the things he knows he should not do, he does. We struggle with remaining, indwelling sin.

This is why Paul says in Philippians 2:12–13: *“Work out your salvation with fear and trembling; for it is God who works in you both to will and to do for His good pleasure.”* We work because God is working in us. But it is work. We are fighting even ourselves as we grow in discernment.

## Challenge 2: Spiritual Influences

While we must fight our fallen natures, we also face external spiritual influences — primarily the devil:

> *“Be sober, be vigilant; because your adversary the devil walks about like a roaring lion, seeking whom he may devour.”* (1 Peter 5:8)

Satan is not particularly concerned with unbelievers — they are already in his camp. His focus is on believers. He wants to keep us confused, because confusion is wonderful from his perspective. He loves a church that is confused about the Word of God, confused about the gospel, unclear on distinctions between good and evil. He loves fuzzy ideas and gray areas, because they distract us from pursuing spiritual discernment.

At times he even appears as an angel of light (2 Corinthians 11:14) — offering something that looks good, looks godly, but is just one degree off from what God says. And one degree off, maintained over a long enough distance, takes you very far from where God wants you.

The way to overcome this challenge is to put on the full armor of God (Ephesians 6) — truth, righteousness, faith, salvation, the Spirit, and the Word of God.

## Challenge 3: Cultural Influences

We must also be prepared to deal with influences arising from the culture in which we live. I want to identify four cultural obstacles to spiritual discernment.

### a. A Secular Worldview

A worldview is a set of presuppositions — call them biases if you like — that we use to interpret everything around us. Everyone has one, whether aware of it or not. As Nancy Pearcey says, a worldview acts like a mental map that tells us how to navigate the world.

Research conducted in 2003 of 233 adults who considered themselves born-again Christians found that fewer than 9 percent of them held a consistently Christian worldview, as measured by just six basic presuppositions: (1) Jesus lived a sinless life; (2) God is all-powerful, all-knowing, and still rules today; (3) salvation is a gift from God and cannot be earned; (4) Satan is real; (5) Christians have a responsibility to share their faith; and (6) the Bible is accurate in all its teachings. Only 9 percent agreed with all six.

The secular worldview we are surrounded by refuses to acknowledge the antithesis between good and evil. We have moved past postmodernism, where the idea was *there is no absolute truth*. We are now in a culture that says: *there is absolute truth, and it is the opposite of what God says*. Evil is called good and good is called evil. This encourages a mindset that rejects discernment as unnecessarily divisive.

### b. A Low View of Scripture

Christians adopt a secular worldview in part because they have a low view of the Scriptures. The battle for inerrancy was largely won in evangelicalism in the twentieth century. But that gave way to an even more dangerous struggle: **the battle for the sufficiency of the Scriptures**.

As James Montgomery Boice wrote in 2000: *“The most serious issue I believe is biblical sufficiency. While Christians are eager to embrace the Bible and treat it as a precious possession, few are willing to give it the preeminence it rightly deserves.”*

We often read and obey the Bible on our own terms — telling the Bible what it can tell us, rather than being governed by it. When we reject the sufficiency of Scripture in practice, we begin to depend on other things: feelings, voices, visions, and other subjective means of supposedly knowing God apart from the Bible. But spiritual discernment must be founded on God’s objective revelation of Himself in Scripture.

### c. A Low View of Theology

David Wells recounts a student who came to him after an introductory theology course and said he had struggled with his conscience about taking it — wondering whether it was right to spend so much money on a theology course so irrelevant to his desire to minister to people. The student was saying that the study of God was irrelevant to ministry.

Perhaps none of us would be so bold as to say that — and yet do we sometimes think it? *Give me something practical, not theology.* But all theology drawn from the Bible is practical, because we are not just hearers of the Word but doers. When theology is downplayed, we are left with a hodgepodge of beliefs that bears little resemblance to biblical Christianity — and the pursuit of discernment becomes even harder.

### d. A Low View of God's Holiness

When the Bible says God is holy, the primary thing it is saying is that God is *not us*. He is unlike us. He is completely other. We tend to think of God as us, just bigger and more powerful. But God is not us at all. That is why we needed the Son to come in the flesh, so we could relate to God. That is why we needed the Bible. If our view of God is small and domesticated — a genie in a lamp who exists to serve our desires — we will not grow in spiritual discernment.

## Conclusion

We face real challenges in pursuing discernment: internal influences from our fallen natures, spiritual influences from the devil, and cultural influences — a secular worldview, a low view of Scripture, a low view of theology, and a low view of God’s holiness.

But God has not left us without resources. He has given us His Spirit dwelling within us. He has given us the full armor of God. He has given us His sufficient and inerrant Word. Growing in discernment is difficult, but it is the calling of every Christian — and God is working in us to accomplish it.
